parameters.jsonBody: ={{ JSON.stringify({ type:'SMS', contactId: $('Get Contact (Entry)').first().json.contact.id, message: "{{contact.first_name}}, here's something worth knowing \u2014 if your home was built or renovated before 2007, there's a good chance it doesn't meet the current Florida hurricane protection code. That doesn't mean it's unsafe. But there's a gap most homeowners don't find out about until they file a claim.\n\nOur guide covers exactly what to check. Reply with your email and I'll send it right over.", fromNumber: "+19542808890" }) }}

parameters.jsonBody:
={{ JSON.stringify({ type:'Email', contactId: $('Get Contact (Entry)').first().json.contact.id, subject: "Good meeting you today, {{contact.first_name}}", html: " Reece Windows & Doors
Something I noticed about the homes on your street ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌
Reece Windows & Doors

Hi {{contact.first_name}},

Good meeting you today. I know a knock on the door isn’t the most expected thing, so I appreciate you taking a minute.

Here’s what I didn’t get to mention while we were chatting: a lot of the homes in {{contact.city}} were built or renovated before the current Florida building code took effect. That doesn’t automatically mean there’s a problem — but there’s one detail about your home’s hurricane protection that determines everything from insurance eligibility to actual storm safety.

Most homeowners don’t know about it until something goes wrong.

Over the next couple of days, I’ll share what that detail is and how to check it yourself in about 5 minutes. No appointment needed.

Talk soon,

{{custom_values.rep_name}}

Reece Windows & Doors
Protecting families since 1972
{{location.address}}, {{location.city}}, {{location.state}} {{location.postal_code}}
Unsubscribe  •  © {{right_now.year}} {{location.name}}
", from: "randy@getreecewindows.com", fromName: "Randy Reece" }) }}

nodes.name: Send SMS: SMS #1: Standalone Emotional Hook

parameters.jsonBody: ={{ JSON.stringify({ type:'SMS', contactId: $('Get Contact (Entry)').first().json.contact.id, message: "Hey {{contact.first_name}}, quick question \u2014 do you know what your windows are actually rated for? It's the number one thing that determines whether your insurance claim gets paid after a storm. Most homeowners have no idea.", fromNumber: "+19542808890" }) }}

nodes.name: Send Email: Email #2: Neighborhood Risk Awareness

parameters.jsonBody:
={{ JSON.stringify({ type:'Email', contactId: $('Get Contact (Entry)').first().json.contact.id, subject: "What we noticed walking your neighborhood", html: " Reece Windows & Doors
The gap between "passed inspection" & "fully protected"  ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌
Reece Windows & Doors

Hi {{contact.first_name}},

Yesterday I mentioned there’s one detail about your home’s hurricane protection that most homeowners miss. Here’s the part that matters most for homes on your street.

Your insurance carrier doesn’t grade your coverage based on when your home was built. They grade it based on what your windows can prove right now — the certification, the wind zone rating, and whether they meet your jurisdiction’s current code.

Most homeowners assume that if their home passed inspection, they’re covered. That’s the biggest misconception we see.

Last hurricane season, a family in Broward thought their impact windows had them covered. They filed a $45,000 claim and it was denied. The windows were rated for the wrong wind zone. Installed by a licensed contractor. Looked right. Passed the eye test. But the paperwork didn’t match.

Here’s the part that makes it worse: there’s no system that notifies you when your home’s classification drops. No annual check. No letter from your insurer. Most homeowners only discover the gap when they file a claim — and by then, it’s too late to fix it affordably.

I’m not saying your home has this issue. But Florida has raised the hurricane protection standard multiple times since most homes in {{contact.city}} were built. What passed then doesn’t pass now — not because anything broke, but because the bar moved.

I’m putting together something for you that breaks this down step by step — how to check your home’s classification yourself in about 5 minutes. I’ll send it over tomorrow.

Talk soon,

{{custom_values.rep_name}}

Reece Windows & Doors
Protecting families since 1972
{{location.address}}, {{location.city}}, {{location.state}} {{location.postal_code}}
Unsubscribe  •  © {{right_now.year}} {{location.name}}
", from: "randy@getreecewindows.com", fromName: "Randy Reece" }) }}

nodes.name: Send SMS: SMS #2: Code Compliance Urgency Seed

parameters.jsonBody: ={{ JSON.stringify({ type:'SMS', contactId: $('Get Contact (Entry)').first().json.contact.id, message: "{{contact.first_name}}, one thing that caught a lot of homeowners off guard last year \u2014 Florida raised the hurricane protection requirements again. Homes that were fully compliant a few years ago might not be anymore. Quick thing to check before season hits.", fromNumber: "+19542808890" }) }}
